transcription.transcript:
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing different plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies. I wear layers, use hiking poles, boots, and carry a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other tasks afterward.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax. We usually have periods of chatting and periods of silence to take it all in.

Interviewer: How does hiking alone compare to hiking with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I go without my phone, other times I listen to podcasts or talks.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, usually spring to fall, not in winter.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: Typically two to three hours this year, though in past years I’ve done longer hikes up to six hours or more.

Interviewer: How many hikes do you do annually?

Participant: Probably 20 to 30 hikes a year, mostly on weekends from spring to fall.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half are familiar places I visit yearly, and half are new locations I try to explore.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same hiking spots?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and proximity is a factor since they’re close by. I also like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress over time.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences.

Interviewer: When hiking alone, how do you manage safety concerns?

Participant: I take precautions by only hiking trails I know from previous group hikes. I avoid new or unfamiliar trails alone and stick to shorter, well-traveled paths where I’m likely to see others. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family so they know where I am and when to expect me back.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use any other apps to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera a lot to capture scenes or plants I want to paint or identify later. I also use a Fitbit to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Do you use any other devices on hikes?

Participant: No, just my phone and Fitbit.

Interviewer: What is your main purpose for using the camera on hikes?

Participant: To capture beautiful views, interesting patterns, animals, or plants I want to identify or paint later.

Interviewer: How do your photos help you connect back to the places you’ve hiked?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of when and where I was. Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just written notes.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other hiking devices?

Participant: My phone and Fitbit cover my needs, and I’m not sure what else is available. If I learn about something useful, I might try it.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, do you think you could hike safely alone?

Participant: Somewhat. I’d probably stick to shorter, well-populated trails and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel as confident going farther without location tracking.

Interviewer: What are your preferences regarding hike difficulty?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time availability is the biggest factor. If I have a full day, I enjoy longer hikes—up to about 16.5 miles. In summer, a typical hike is around six or seven miles. Shorter hikes are enjoyable too, but longer ones are preferred when possible.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown and require some bushwhacking, but mostly I stay on the path.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery. Did you use it on your last hike?

Participant: No, I didn’t need it on the hike but used it while driving back.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old. On longer hikes, I might need to recharge. It’s also a precaution in case something happens and I need to keep my phone or flashlight powered.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for longer ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record your hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of how I used it. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes, which could be interesting.

Interviewer: Did using the device take away from your experience in nature?

Participant: Somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband was using the same device, it felt natural. We reminded each other when to record. It felt a bit strange when other people saw me talking to myself, though.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay hiking?

Participant: Yes. On the last hike, we had to get back for an errand, so we checked the time to make sure we left in time.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Unfortunately, yes.

Interviewer: Could you hike without checking the time and still enjoy it?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time when you have an appointment would be more stressful. But if I don’t have somewhere to be, I get a better experience without worrying about time. Sometimes I check the time just to be aware of the heat of the day.

Interviewer: You used the map a few times on a trail you know well. Why?

Participant: The trail is less used and sometimes overgrown, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS connection, so I check to ensure it’s recording properly.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating.

Interviewer: We appreciate your time. We’ll reach out when the gift card is ready.

Participant: Thank you. Have a good evening.

Interviewer: You too. Goodbye.

Participant: Bye.

Interviewer: Okay. So, before we get into your hiking experience for last week, I want to find out from you what generally hiking to you, what does hiking mean to you generally?

Participant: I think it's a way I enjoy to do a lot of different things that are good for me. I think I like to be outdoors and be away from the computer and from work and to kind of relax and think about things without having kind of all the constant noise. I like to see different plants and animals and views. I like to paint, so a lot of times I'll take pictures of things I see on hikes and paint them.

Interviewer: Okay. And generally when you're going out on a hike, what are some of the things you kind of use or how do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I try to have enough food and water and first aid needs, kind of practical things like that and make sure I have kind of layers of clothes and my hiking poles and boots and backpack and things like that. And then I will try to make sure I have my phone all the way charged and I usually bring like a little extra battery pack that I can use for my phone or for like a flashlight just in case.

Interviewer: Okay. But what will you say are some of the benefits you gain from hiking?

Participant: Definitely getting more physically fit. I think it helps me to get stronger and have better cardio health. I like to, when I can hike with other people, I really enjoy that. I think it's a good time to kind of talk and bond with people and do that together. And I think I just always feel better mentally from hiking too. Just kind of, I feel refreshed and calmer and ready to go do the other things I need to do.

Interviewer: Okay. But when you're on a hike and you are with friends and chatting, doesn't that draw you away from the objective of going to hike that will help you feel refreshed and re-motivate you again?

Participant: I guess maybe at times I probably am more focusing on the conversation than I'm, in that case I probably am missing being fully focused on some of the things I'm seeing. But I think a lot of times since the things we're talking about aren't necessarily about the things that I'm kind of stressed about and trying to relax from, I do feel like that also helps me. And usually when I'm with other people, sometimes I go by myself and sometimes I go with friends. We're usually not talking the whole time. So there's kind of periods where we're chatting and periods where we're just in silence taking it in.

Interviewer: Okay. And what will you say will be the difference between you hiking alone and you hiking with friends? Are the impacts different when you are hiking alone and when you're hiking with group?

Participant: Yeah. I think when I'm by myself, I tend to think a lot more and I probably notice my surroundings more. Sometimes when I hike alone, I'll just go without kind of having my phone or anything. And sometimes I will listen to a podcast or a talk or something like that for part of it.

Interviewer: Okay. And how frequently do you go on a hike?

Participant: Pretty much every weekend when it's nice out.

Interviewer: Okay. Not in the winter.

Participant: Okay.

Interviewer: And how long do you usually spend out hiking?

Participant: Probably a couple hours. I'd say this year I've been a bit more busy, so not as much as in past years. Probably this year, like two to three hours. Previous years have been like up to six or more probably.

Interviewer: Okay. And what kind of your average number of times you hike probably in a year?

Participant: Let's see. Like math in my head here. Probably if I start hiking like the end of March. I guess if I'm hiking every week, maybe like 20 to 30 maybe.

Interviewer: Okay. Every weekend and then spring to fall. And are they usually the same places you hike or you have different locations?

Participant: A lot of different locations. Usually there's some that I will usually do like each year. But I also try to look for new places. So probably about half and half new versus old places.

Interviewer: Okay. And you hiking those places, is there probably a motivation that would make you go back to those places and hike there again? And what would those motivations be?

Participant: Oh, sorry. Go ahead. I interrupted you.

Interviewer: Yeah. What would those motivations be that would push you to hike the same place probably twice or thrice?

Participant: Okay. That's a good question. I think when it's a place that I'm coming back to year after year, a lot of times it's just because I enjoy it so much. And it's I guess at a certain point I've kind of hiked a lot of places that are within close driving distance. So that's definitely a factor that will make me if it's close. And also it's fun. There are a couple of shorter hikes that I will often do several times in a year because for those I enjoy getting to see how the hike changes through the seasons. Like in the spring when the little plants are just coming up and in the fall the leaves changing. And it's also fun to see my own progress. Like usually by the end of the hiking season I'm stronger and I can hike it more easily. So that's fun too.

Interviewer: Okay. And does the time changes and the seasons affect your well-being when you hike?

Participant: Probably. I think it's kind of, I don't know, I would say there's not necessarily one season that's necessarily better. But I think I enjoy seeing just the different things there are to appreciate in each season or time.

Interviewer: Okay. And do you, when hiking alone, what will you say about safetyness? Because usually when you're hiking with a group, you might say you're usually safe with your group because you're all together. But when you're hiking alone, how safe are you and how worried are you with your surrounding?

Participant: I think I try to take some precautions when I go by myself because, yeah, it is a bit more dangerous. So I will only go to a hike that I have already done with a group pretty much. If I'm going by myself, I don't really usually go somewhere completely new that I'm not familiar with the trail. And I try to stick to hikes that are a bit shorter and that are well-traveled so that I'll usually see a lot of other hikers and families and people. And something that I also like to do is I have an app called Strava and it has a feature that you can send a link to your GPS location to people. So I will usually send, if I'm going by myself, I'll send a link to that to like my, probably usually my husband and my mom so that they know where I am and I'll tell them when I'm going to get back. So if I don't come back, they can have a way to

Interviewer: start finding out what happened.

Participant: Okay.

Interviewer: And aside the Strava, is there any other apps you use while hiking that kind of helps you to connect with nature and then stay focused on your hiking?

Participant: I guess the phone camera, does that count?

Interviewer: That's probably...

Participant: Yeah.

Interviewer: Yes.

Participant: Yeah, that's probably the other one that I use. I guess... I don't know if this counts either, but I have like a Fitbit watch thing. So sometimes I'll look at like how many steps that it's counted or like my heart rate or things like that on there.

Interviewer: Yeah, the Fitbit is a device, so definitely it counts since it's a device and it's a technological device which has been built for use. It counts.

Participant: Okay.

Interviewer: And then aside the Fitbit and your cell phone, do you have any other devices you probably use generally when you're going on a hike?

Participant: No, not really.

Interviewer: Okay. And with your phone, you did mention of the Strava and also the camera that you use. What are the main focus for using the camera? I know you've spoken about the Strava to send out GPS tracking to families to track you since you're alone. But what of the camera?

Participant: I think a couple main reasons. Sometimes I just will see something that's pretty like a nice view or maybe kind of an interesting like pattern in the rocks or maybe an animal or something and I'll take pictures of those so I can remember it and also so I can paint it. I like to paint from my photos that I take. And then the other reason is probably maybe if there's a I like to identify plants. So if I see one and I don't know what it is, I will take a picture of it and then maybe when I get home, I will try to use that to look it up on the internet and find out the name of the plant.

Interviewer: Okay. And you spoke about taking pictures of things around you that's will kind of give you a memory of it afterwards. How do those memory or pictures help you to connect back to the places you've hiked?

Participant: I think I like having I upload my pictures to like Google Photos or something and that will often like kind of send you reminders of oh, you were here at this time. So I like to look back on it in that way. And sometimes I also just like to look back and kind of flip through my old pictures and remember different locations or different experiences. I think having the picture kind of helps bring my memory back to being there more so than just kind of maybe having it written down would.

Interviewer: Okay. What of the devices you take aside the smartphone and Fitbit? Why don't you use any other device to hike?

Participant: I guess probably number one is I feel like those pretty much cover the needs that I have and I guess number two is I'm not I guess I'm not really sure what else there would be out there to use. So if I learned about something new that I thought could help me have a better experience, I might try it out.

Interviewer: Okay. And without these apps, this is Strava and camera, do you think you can be safe hiking alone and why?

Participant: I think sort of. I think I would probably not go to some places if I didn't have like the location feature because that makes me I think I would definitely try to stick to smaller places and places with more people and just be out for a shorter amount of time. I don't think I would feel as confident in going farther away.

Interviewer: Okay. This probably could be a more kind of a general way of asking you about your preferences for hiking. Is it hike, difficult hike? What are your preferences for hiking actually?

Participant: I like hikes that are pretty challenging. I think the biggest factor is how much time do I have? If I have a lot of time, I would like to go on longer ones. I think probably the longest distance I have gone is about like 16 and a half miles. And if I have time for that, I really enjoy the longer ones. Probably like a normal hike for like the middle of the summer would be around six or seven miles. But I enjoy shorter ones too. But if I have a whole day off or something, I would prefer to go on a longer one.

Interviewer: And how would you define your longer hike?

Participant: Probably like eight or more miles is longer for me.

Interviewer: Okay. Okay. All right. So this is the hike, the hiking trail you went. Let me pull up the 3D version of it as well. Just for us to have a clearer view of it. I need to have a clearer view of your 3D. Just a second. Let me pull up your hike you went on.

Participant: If it was just the time it would be my Fitbit.

Interviewer: Okay.

Participant: Yeah. I think usually when I use my phone to look at the GPS I probably will see what the time is just when I pick up the device. But if I was just only checking the time I would just look at my Fitbit.

Participant: Okay.

Interviewer: And also during our conversation earlier on you made mention of you taking pictures but in here we realized you took pictures just twice.

Participant: Yeah. I would say that's probably because it was mostly in the trees most of the time so I took it when we had a lookout point but I think I took one there and then I took another one in the trees of a plant that I was trying to identify.

Interviewer: Okay. You've been in the trees actually. How does it feel to be in an environment which is far from human living environment?

Participant: I really enjoy it. I think there are a lot of interesting things to notice and to see because it's very alive with all different kinds of plants and animals and it's just I don't know everything is different. There's different sounds and different things to see, different smells even. So I think it's because everything is different from my normal day-to-day experience. I feel that I notice a lot more things about my environment.

Interviewer: Okay. And talking about let's come back to your idea of having a hike which is a bit more challenging. Will you prefer hiking on trails or going through a path of your own?

Participant: I think generally I try to stick to trails that are already created just because I think it helps to kind of preserve the environment a bit more than if I were to kind of go all over the place. Sometimes I have been on trails where the trail is pretty overgrown so you kind of have to just hack your way through it. But for the most part I try to stay to where the trail is and I think also just for safety usually the trail provides a safe way.

Interviewer: In the beginning of the interview you also made mention of you carrying a long battery. Did you use it on this hike?

Participant: I actually didn't use it on the hike. I ended up using it while we were driving back but I didn't use my charge on my phone enough to need it.

Interviewer: And why would you carry an extra battery with you when you're hiking? Usually you could just charge your phone and then go through the hiking experience and then come back. But why would you want to carry an extra battery with you?

Participant: I think two main reasons. The first one is just that my phone is kind of old and so the battery will drain pretty quickly so sometimes especially if I'm on a longer hike sometimes the battery will need to be recharged. And then the other reason is just that as more of a precautionary thing like if I were to have something happen and not be able to return quickly I would want to be able to have more battery power like in case I needed to continue navigating or try to keep it alive to get to somewhere that I could call. I can also use that charger to like charge my flashlight that I bring so if I were to need that as well.

Interviewer: So generally would you say an extra battery is necessary for hiking?

Participant: Probably not for a short hike but for a long hike I'd say I guess if not necessary is very very useful.

Interviewer: Okay and with the hiking device you used for the recording how was your experience like with the device? Did it distract you from your hiking experience? Did it kind of you finding difficulty using it while hiking and how was your experience like generally actually?

Participant: I don't think it made a huge difference. But I think it definitely made me more conscious of how I was using it.

Interviewer: Sorry yeah continue.

Participant: Oh and I guess it did kind of make me wonder a little bit like what it like that it it made me think it might be interesting to kind of record just maybe even like a little hike journal or like take kind of notes about just things that I'm seeing or have like a little record of my hike in that way. But yeah.

Interviewer: Okay and it making you feel conscious of your hiking and your device usage don't you think that it's kind of drew you from that experience you needed in nature with your device usage?

Participant: Probably somewhat yeah.

Interviewer: Yeah.

Interviewer: Okay. Okay. And with with this device you were hiking with others around you how comfortable it was using the device?

Participant: hiking with other people. Well, because I was hiking with my husband, who is also using the same device, I didn't, I don't know, we were both kind of doing it so it didn't really feel that weird. We'd like remind each other if we were going to do something and needed to record it, but it did, I don't know, it did feel a little bit strange sometimes if I saw other people on the trail and they kind of look at me talking to myself and be a little confused, but.

Interviewer: Okay. All right. And then you taking pictures and checking your time, specifically checking your time. Did you checking your time probably once in a while affect how you wanted to stay out hiking? Did it affect it anyway?

Participant: Yes. Yeah, actually, especially on this last hike, because we realized partway through that my husband needed to run an errand to somewhere, and we realized that in order to get him back in time, we would need to have a certain amount of time to head back down. So we were checking the time to make sure we could turn around in order to get him back on time.

Interviewer: Okay. Do you think that probably checking the time because you have an appointment to meet can distract you from this wellness you are seeking for in nature?

Participant: Yes. Yeah, unfortunately.

Interviewer: And do you think probably there'll be a better way to achieve this goal, probably going up in nature without checking the time? Is there going to be a possibility of that happening, going up hiking without checking the time and you getting back?

Participant: I think it probably depends on a couple things. I think, I guess maybe I should, when I said earlier, I guess I should say I think it probably would have been more stressful to know that we had an appointment and to not know what time it was. So that probably would have been even more distracting because then I would just worry about it and not be able to know. But I think I definitely do get a better experience if I don't have somewhere to be and I'm able to not have to worry about getting back in time. I think sometimes in those cases I check the time, not necessarily so I can return home at a certain time, but more so I can be aware of how the heat of the day will work. So maybe if I can see, oh, it's before noon, then I can assume it will probably get hotter than it is now. But if it's a bit later, then I can assume that it will get cooler.

Interviewer: Okay. What of the map usage? I think you did mention that this is a trail you've used a lot of times in your kind of history, but you kind of used the map a couple of times while on the trail.

Participant: Yeah.

Interviewer: Why?

Participant: Oh, sorry. Go ahead.

Interviewer: Yeah, I think you got it. I wanted to ask why you kind of used it when you already know the place.

Participant: Yeah, I think probably two main reasons. One being that this is a trail that is used a little bit less. And so there's some parts where there's a lot of plants and it's maybe a little bit difficult to make sure that you get the right turn. I just want to make sure. And then also the other reason that because my phone doesn't work very well, sometimes it will lose the GPS connection if I don't check the map. summary.short_summary: The participant views hiking as a beneficial outdoor activity that promotes physical fitness, mental relaxation, and creative inspiration through photography. They prepare carefully with essential gear and technology like a phone, extra battery, and Fitbit, and balance hiking alone and with friends to enjoy both solitude and social bonding. They prefer established trails, appreciate seasonal changes, and use apps like Strava for safety and connection. While technology sometimes distracts, it also enhances their experience by helping track progress and preserve memories.
summary.long_summary: The participant describes hiking as a multifaceted activity that supports their physical health, mental well-being, and artistic pursuits. They hike frequently, mostly on weekends from spring to fall, splitting time between familiar and new trails. Preparation includes layered clothing, food, first aid, hiking poles, and technology such as a phone with GPS and an extra battery, along with a Fitbit for fitness tracking. Hiking alone allows for reflection and close observation of nature, while hiking with friends offers social interaction without significantly detracting from relaxation. They prioritize safety by sticking to known trails, sharing GPS location via Strava, and avoiding unfamiliar paths alone. The participant values the seasonal variety in hiking experiences and prefers challenging hikes when time permits. Although technology like cameras and recording devices occasionally distracts from immersion in nature, it also aids in capturing memories and monitoring progress. Time awareness can sometimes limit the hiking experience, but the participant balances this with practical needs. Overall, hiking is a meaningful, well-prepared activity that blends physical exercise, mental refreshment, social connection, and creative expression.

themes.name: Physical and Mental Health Benefits
themes.description: Hiking is valued for its positive impact on physical fitness and mental well-being, including improved cardio health, relaxation, and mental refreshment.
representative_quotes: It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health.
Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other tasks afterward.

themes.name: Use of Technology for Safety and Documentation
themes.description: The participant relies on technology such as phone apps (Strava), camera, and Fitbit to ensure safety, track progress, and capture moments for later reflection or artistic use.
representative_quotes: I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family so they know where I am and when to expect me back.
I use my phone camera a lot to capture scenes or plants I want to paint or identify later.
I also use a Fitbit to track steps and heart rate.

themes.name: Social vs. Solo Hiking Experiences
themes.description: Hiking with others offers social bonding and conversation, while solo hiking allows for deeper reflection and better noticing of surroundings; both have distinct benefits and occasional trade-offs.
representative_quotes: I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond.
When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better.
Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and miss some sights.

themes.name: Preference for Established Trails and Seasonal Variation
themes.description: The participant prefers established trails for safety and environmental reasons, appreciates seasonal changes for unique experiences, and balances familiar and new locations.
representative_quotes: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety.
I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences.
About half are familiar places I visit yearly, and half are new locations I try to explore.

themes.name: Impact of Time Awareness on Hiking Experience
themes.description: Checking time and managing duration can detract from the relaxation benefits of hiking, but is necessary when balancing other commitments or environmental conditions.
representative_quotes: Checking the time distracts from the wellness you seek in nature.
If I don’t have somewhere to be, I get a better experience without worrying about time.
On the last hike, we had to get back for an errand, so we checked the time to make sure we left in time.

themes.name: Preparation and Safety Precautions
themes.description: The participant prepares carefully with food, water, layers, and extra battery packs, avoids unfamiliar trails when alone, and uses technology to enhance safety.
representative_quotes: I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies.
I avoid new or unfamiliar trails alone and stick to shorter, well-traveled paths.
My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old. On longer hikes, I might need to recharge.

highlights.quote: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise.
highlights.rationale: This quote captures the participant's emotional connection to hiking as a form of mental relaxation and escape from daily stress.
highlights.suggested_use: Use in wellness or mental health research to illustrate the emotional benefits of outdoor activities.

highlights.quote: I take precautions by only hiking trails I know from previous group hikes. I avoid new or unfamiliar trails alone and stick to shorter, well-traveled paths where I’m likely to see others.
highlights.rationale: This quote details specific safety behaviors the participant uses when hiking alone, showing risk management strategies.
highlights.suggested_use: Highlight in safety-focused studies or app design for solo hikers to understand user safety practices.

highlights.quote: Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just written notes.
highlights.rationale: This quote emphasizes how visual documentation enhances memory and connection to hiking experiences.
highlights.suggested_use: Use in research on memory aids, digital journaling, or the role of photography in nature engagement.

highlights.quote: Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other tasks afterward.
highlights.rationale: This succinctly expresses the mental health benefits of hiking, including stress relief and renewed focus.
highlights.suggested_use: In presentations or papers about the psychological benefits of outdoor exercise.

highlights.quote: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax.
highlights.rationale: This quote reveals the participant's awareness of how social interaction during hiking affects their mindfulness and relaxation.
highlights.suggested_use: Use in studies exploring social dynamics and mindfulness during outdoor activities.

highlights.quote: I carry an extra battery for my phone or flashlight just in case. It’s a precaution in case something happens and I need to keep my phone or flashlight powered.
highlights.rationale: Shows proactive preparation behavior to ensure safety and connectivity during hikes.
highlights.suggested_use: Inform design of hiking gear checklists or safety education materials.

highlights.quote: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of how I used it.
highlights.rationale: Highlights how technology use can alter user behavior and awareness during hiking.
highlights.suggested_use: In research on technology’s impact on outdoor experiences and user interaction with recording devices.

highlights.quote: Checking the time distracts from the wellness you seek in nature, but sometimes it’s necessary depending on appointments or heat of the day.
highlights.rationale: Expresses the tension between practical needs and emotional immersion in nature.
highlights.suggested_use: Use in studies about time awareness and its effect on relaxation during outdoor activities.

clarifying: You mentioned that sometimes you hike without your phone and other times you listen to podcasts or talks. Can you clarify what factors influence your choice between these options?
When you said you usually bring an extra battery pack for your phone or flashlight just in case, can you clarify if you use the same battery pack for both devices or do you carry separate ones?
probing: You said hiking helps you feel refreshed and calmer mentally. Can you describe a specific moment on a hike when you felt this mental refresh most strongly?
You mentioned that checking the time sometimes distracts you from the wellness you seek in nature. Could you elaborate on how time awareness impacts your overall hiking experience?
contrast: How does your experience of noticing surroundings and thinking more deeply when hiking alone compare to the social bonding and conversation benefits you get when hiking with friends?
You mentioned that seasonal changes offer unique experiences but no one season is better. Could you compare how your physical and mental benefits from hiking differ across these seasons?

recommendations: Maintain the clear distinction between short and long summaries as currently done.
Continue providing representative quotes for each theme to support thematic analysis.
Ensure follow-up questions remain aligned with participant responses for deeper insights.
Keep the transcript formatting consistent and well-structured for readability.
Consider minor proofreading for typographical consistency (e.g., 'memotional' category typo in quotes section).
